elaboration.definition: The active construction of meaning—connecting ideas, explaining why things work, and organizing what you know into a coherent structure, not merely holding isolated facts.
elaboration.examples:
Explaining why supply and demand curves slope the way they do, not just memorizing the diagram
Connecting Newton's laws to predict motion in a scenario you have never seen before
Teaching a concept to someone else and revising your explanation when they get stuck

elaboration.definition: Demonstrated competence—the ability to apply, perform, or solve reliably in context, beyond knowing that something is true.
elaboration.examples:
Playing a piano piece without stopping to recall fingerings
Debugging unfamiliar code using first principles instead of pattern-matching
Diagnosing a case when the presentation does not match the textbook example

elaboration.definition: What you hold as true or can state—facts, definitions, propositions, and information accumulated over time.
elaboration.examples:
Knowing that Paris is the capital of France
Recalling the formula for compound interest
Listing the stages of mitosis in order

elaboration.definition: The deliberate pursuit of questions—examining assumptions, gathering evidence, and revising beliefs through investigation rather than accepting answers passively.
elaboration.examples:
Asking what would disprove a claim before accepting it
Designing an experiment to test a hypothesis
Reading primary sources after a lecture to check the summary

elaboration.definition: The set of cognitive capacities—attention, working memory, long-term memory, reasoning—that together make thought and understanding possible.
elaboration.examples:
Working memory limits how many ideas you can hold in relation at once
Attention determines what gets encoded and what is filtered out
Long-term memory stores conceptual structures that can be retrieved and extended

elaboration.definition: Taking in information without active processing—skimming headlines, scrolling feeds, or watching content without integrating it into your own model of how things work.
elaboration.examples:
Reading ten article summaries and remembering none of the theses
Watching a tutorial, feeling like you learned, then failing to apply it
Highlighting a PDF without rewriting the ideas in your own words
